questions.scenario: The team adds version 4 of `orders-db-url` and immediately disables the old database credential. Some old Cloud Run revisions still use the previous value. What went wrong?

options.text: Rotation was treated as only a secret update instead of a release plan with rollout and verification.
Secret Manager cannot store more than one version.
Cloud Run revisions never use configuration.
KMS automatically changes app traffic splits.

questions.explanation: Rotation must account for the dependency, secret version, app rollout, verification, and safe old-credential cleanup.

questions.scenario: A reviewer asks for proof that a secret was rotated. Which evidence is safe to share?

options.text: Version metadata, IAM review, deployment evidence, audit logs, and successful app checks without showing the payload.
The plaintext old and new values in a ticket.
A screenshot of someone's local `.env` file.
The production database password in chat.

questions.explanation: Secret evidence should prove state and behavior without exposing the sensitive value.

questions.scenario: Which statements about encryption and KMS match the article?

options.text: Secret Manager encrypts secret data in transit and at rest.
IAM still controls who can read the payload.
CMEK with Cloud KMS is an extra control when requirements justify it.
Encryption means every principal should be able to read every secret.

questions.explanation: Encryption, IAM, audit, and rotation solve different parts of the secret problem. Encryption does not justify broad access.
